1.exposition: Paul turns from the citizen’s duties toward the state to the congregation’s own inner life, and to a quarrel running along the seam of the Roman church — Jew and Gentile, the scrupulous and the free. His first word is not a ruling but a welcome: receive the one who is weak in the faith, and not for the settling of his doubts by argument. One believes he may eat all things; another, weaker, eats only vegetables — and each is tempted to his own fault, the strong to despise, the weak to judge. Paul cuts under both: God has received him, and to his own master he stands or falls. The same holds for the keeping of days (v.5): let each be fully persuaded, for both the observer and the eater act unto the Lord with thanksgiving. Then the argument lifts from etiquette to lordship — none of us lives or dies to himself; we are the Lord’s, and to this end Christ died and rose, that he might be Lord of the dead and living. So the mutual judging must cease, for we shall all stand before the judgment seat, and each shall give account of himself to God.
1.original_language: The party names set the terms. The “weak” is ho asthenōn tē pistei — weak not in trust but with respect to faith, that is, in the grasp of what his faith sets him free to do; Paul later calls the other side hoi dynatoi, “the able, the strong” (15:1). To “receive” is proslambanō, to take a person to oneself, welcome into full fellowship — not a grudging toleration. “Doubtful disputations” renders diakriseis dialogismōn, wranglings over the weak man’s misgivings. And the two besetting sins are precisely paired: the strong exoutheneō (“treat as nothing, despise”) the weak, while the weak krinō (“judge, condemn”) the strong.
1.historical_context: The dispute is the Jew–Gentile fault line that has run beneath the whole letter. Jewish believers kept the food laws and the sacred calendar — passover, new moons, the ceremonial sabbaths; Gentile believers regarded these as indifferent matters no longer binding. So the “weak in faith” are not doubters of the gospel but the scrupulous, whose conscience is not yet free of Torah’s table and calendar. The abstinence from flesh may also reflect the fear that market-meat had been offered to idols, a worry Paul handles from a kindred angle in 1 Corinthians 8. Paul himself stands with the strong (15:1) — but he writes to check their contempt, not to arm it.
1.christ: The horizontal quarrel is dissolved by a vertical Lord. Christ died and rose again that he might be Lord both of the dead and the living: his cross and resurrection purchased a dominion over every believer, so that each member belongs to Christ and answers to Christ, not to the other members. And the whole of our petty judging is set under the last assize — we shall all stand before the judgment seat. The weightier text reads “the judgment seat of God,” and the very next verse proves the point by an oath spoken of Yahweh in Isaiah 45:23 now applied without apology to the tribunal of Christ — a quiet witness to his deity, and a summons to leave the verdicts to him.

verses.note: The chapter opens with a command, not a concession: him that is weak in the faith receive ye, but not to doubtful disputations. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson identify the “weak” not as weak in trust but as the believer — chiefly the Jewish convert — whose faith has not yet grasped his full liberty, still scrupulous over meats and days, yet truly Christ’s. To “receive” (proslambanō) is to welcome him into full fellowship; eastern Chrysostom and reformed Calvin add the manner — not to drag him into wrangling over his opinions or to unsettle his conscience by dispute. Welcome him; do not argue him.

One believeth that he may eat all things: another, who is weak, eateth herbs. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson explain the strong man’s persuasion that, the ceremonial distinctions being abolished, every food is lawful; the weak, doubtful whether flesh might be unclean or idol-tainted, plays safe with vegetables. eastern Theodoret notes the abstinence sprang from conscience — the concrete, everyday matter of the dinner table in which forbearance was actually needed.

Each party has its besetting sin, and Paul names both: let not him that eateth despise him that eateth not, and let not him which eateth not judge him that eateth. grammatical-historical Robertson marks the pair — the strong tempted to contempt, looking down on the weak as backward; the weak to condemnation, writing off the strong as lawless. The ground silences both: reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret press “for God hath received him” — since God has welcomed both, neither dare reject whom God accepts.

Who art thou that judgest another man’s servant? grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson catch the force: the believer is God’s household servant, not ours; to sit in judgment on a fellow servant is to usurp the Master’s bench. “To his own master he standeth or falleth” — the verdict is God’s alone. And Paul turns the warning into comfort: reformed Calvin hears in “he shall be holden up, for God is able to make him stand” a gracious promise that God’s own power keeps the weak brother from falling.

One man esteemeth one day above another: another esteemeth every day alike. grammatical-historical Barnes and Gill, with reformed Calvin, take the days as the Jewish festival calendar — passover, new moons, ceremonial sabbaths — which the weak still kept as sacred; and Barnes argues strenuously that the verse does not touch the Lord’s Day, observed by all Christians (1 Corinthians 16:2; Acts 20:7). wesleyan-arminian Clarke protests that “alike” is not in the Greek and that the weekly Sabbath abides. Beneath the debate stands Paul’s rule: “let every man be fully persuaded in his own mind.”

He that regardeth the day, regardeth it unto the Lord … he that eateth, eateth to the Lord, for he giveth God thanks. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson find the deciding word: the observer and the non-observer, the eater and the abstainer, act toward the same Lord and both give thanks. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret draw the charity — since each sincerely means to honor God, the heart’s direction hallows the act, and neither may be condemned.

For none of us liveth to himself, and no man dieth to himself. grammatical-historical Robertson notes the widening: no Christian is his own — neither his living nor his dying is a private affair. puritan Matthew Henry and reformed Calvin draw the application: since we belong wholly to another, we are to consult not our own pleasure but our Lord — and here, to bear with a brother rather than please ourselves.

Whether we live, we live unto the Lord; and whether we die, we die unto the Lord … we are the Lord’s. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the total consecration of both states; reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret call it the ground of all comfort — in life and in death the believer is Christ’s own property, and therefore neither lives nor dies for himself.

Paul grounds the claim in the cross and the empty tomb: to this end Christ both died, and rose, and revived, that he might be Lord both of the dead and living. grammatical-historical Robertson notes the purpose clause — Christ’s dying and rising secured his dominion over every believer, departed or living. eastern Cyril of Alexandria and reformed Calvin add that a lordship bought at such a price is exactly why the Christian belongs to him in life and death, and dare not usurp over a fellow servant the rule that is Christ’s.

So the judging is folly: why dost thou judge thy brother? or why dost thou set at nought thy brother? for we shall all stand before the judgment seat of Christ. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the double rebuke — “judge” to the weak, “set at nought” to the strong — checked by one tribunal before which both must appear. They observe too that the weightier manuscripts read “judgment seat of God,” and that the next verse’s citation, spoken of Jehovah, is applied here without apology — a quiet witness to Christ’s divine dignity as Judge.

As I live, saith the Lord, every knee shall bow to me, and every tongue shall confess to God — grammatical-historical Robertson identifies the quotation as Isaiah 45:23, God’s own oath that all creation shall render him homage. reformed Calvin draws the bearing: since every knee bows and every tongue gives account to God, the office of judge is his alone, and our judging of a brother is presumption upon it — the more so as the homage Isaiah renders to Jehovah is here rendered to the Christ before whose seat we stand.

So then every one of us shall give account of himself to God. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson draw the personal conclusion — of himself: each answers for his own conduct, not his brother’s. reformed Calvin turns it inward: let a man mind his own walk and leave his brother to God. It is a striking pairing — in a passage forbidding us to judge one another, Paul grounds the prohibition in the fact that we are each, individually, accountable; the coming reckoning is a summons to self-examination, not to censoriousness.

witnesses.point: The “weak” is a true believer, chiefly the Jewish convert whose faith has not yet grasped his full liberty; the days in view are the Jewish ceremonial calendar, not the Christian Lord’s Day.
God has received both the strong and the weak; the weak brother is God’s own servant and shall be upheld, for God is able to make him stand.
Receive the weak, but not to wrangle over his scruples — welcome him without unsettling his conscience by dispute.
“Alike” is not in the Greek of v.5; the weekly Sabbath remains of lasting obligation, distinct from the Jewish ceremonial days the passage sets aside.

external.name: James D.G. Dunn
external.point: Reads the “weak” and “strong” as divided over the Jewish boundary-markers — food laws, sabbaths, feast days — that marked out covenant identity; the quarrel is as much about Jewish and Gentile belonging in one church as about private scruple.

1.application: The community’s task, Paul implies, is to welcome, not to sort — to receive those God has already received, even where their practice differs from ours. Before you make a secondary conviction a test of fellowship, ask whether you are presuming to judge a servant who answers to another Master.

13.pericope_label: Do Not Cause Another to Stumble
13.exposition: The rebuke of judging now becomes a positive charge, and the weight settles onto the strong. Let us not therefore judge one another any more; but judge this rather, that no man put a stumblingblock in his brother’s way — the same verb turned from condemning a brother to resolving for him. Paul concedes the strong man’s principle outright: nothing is unclean of itself — yet to the man who thinks it unclean it is, for he would sin against his conscience. So love, not knowledge, must govern: if thy brother be grieved with thy meat … destroy not him for whom Christ died. For the kingdom of God is not meat and drink, but righteousness, and peace, and joy in the Holy Ghost — the disputed morsel is nothing beside the realities the kingdom is made of. The strong are therefore to follow after the things which make for peace and not for meat destroy the work of God; better to abstain from flesh and wine than to wound a brother. Such faith is best held to oneself before God, for whatsoever is not of faith is sin. And the chapter lifts its eyes at last from the table to the God who is able to establish these very brethren.
13.original_language: The hinge word is koinon — literally “common,” and so “defiled, unclean,” the opposite of what is set apart as clean. Paul’s “nothing unclean of itself” (ouden koinon di’ heautou, v.14) denies any intrinsic defilement in food, while granting that it becomes koinon to the one who reckons it so. In v.17 the kingdom is defined by three nouns — dikaiosynē kai eirēnē kai chara, righteousness, peace, and joy — which in this context carry their relational weight (right dealing and concord among brethren) as much as the deeper graces, all “in the Holy Ghost.” And the goal in v.19 is oikodomē, the “building up” of a house — the brother is a structure to be raised, not a scruple to be crushed.
13.historical_context: Paul’s verdict that “nothing is unclean of itself” assumes the christological end of Torah’s clean/unclean divide — the point dramatized in Peter’s rooftop vision (Acts 10) and in the Lord’s own word that declared all foods clean (Mark 7:19). Yet his move is pastoral before it is doctrinal: the strong are right in principle and can still be wrong in practice, if they press their freedom at the cost of the weak. The measure of that cost is the phrase “him for whom Christ died” (v.15): the over-scrupulous brother is one the Lord counted worth his blood.
13.christ: The cross sets the scale of the whole section. The weak brother is one for whom Christ died — so the strong man’s freedom is not worth the ruin of a soul the Lord bought at that price. The kingdom the section commends is Christ’s own reign in the Spirit (v.17), whose substance is righteousness, peace, and joy, not diet. And the closing doxology names the God who is able to establish the wavering “according to my gospel and the preaching of Jesus Christ” (v.25) — from the blood shed for the weak to the message that finally steadies them, it is Christ who frames the strong man’s duty.

verses.note: Let us not therefore judge one another any more; but judge this rather, that no man put a stumblingblock or an occasion to fall in his brother’s way. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson relish the wordplay — instead of judging the brother, “judge” (resolve, determine) this for him. The address now narrows to the strong: wesleyan-arminian Wesley and eastern Theodoret warn that their liberty must not become the very thing over which the weak stumbles against conscience, or falls.

I know, and am persuaded by the Lord Jesus, that there is nothing unclean of itself. grammatical-historical Barnes and reformed Calvin note that Paul here sides with the strong — the ceremonial distinctions are gone, and every food is pure in its own nature. Yet the same verse guards the weak: “to him that esteemeth any thing to be unclean, to him it is unclean.” eastern Chrysostom presses the pastoral point — a conscience, even when mistaken, binds until better taught; force the weak to eat against it and you make him sin.

Now the governing rule: if thy brother be grieved with thy meat, now walkest thou not charitably. Destroy not him with thy meat, for whom Christ died. grammatical-historical Barnes and reformed Calvin read “destroy” as the ruinous tendency of such conduct — a real danger held over the strong, though the elect, being Christ’s, will be kept by God. wesleyan-arminian Clarke and Wesley take it at full strength: one for whom Christ died may actually be lost, which to them proves both the breadth of the atonement and the possibility of final falling. Either way the argument is one and unanswerable — to wound over a plate of food a soul Christ thought worth his blood is a monstrous breach of love.

Let not then your good be evil spoken of. grammatical-historical Barnes, Robertson, and Ellicott take “your good” as the genuine blessing of Christian liberty; the caution is that the strong not so use it — provoking strife and offense — that a good thing earns a bad name. eastern Theodoret adds that it is better to forgo the exercise of liberty than to bring it, or the gospel itself, into disrepute.

For the kingdom of God is not meat and drink; but righteousness, and peace, and joy in the Holy Ghost. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson insist true religion does not consist in rules about the table; its substance lies elsewhere. On what that substance is, the witnesses differ in emphasis: Barnes, Ellicott, and (here) reformed Calvin take the three in the practical sense the context invites — right dealing, concord, and gladness among the brethren, wrought by the Spirit — while grammatical-historical Gill and wesleyan-arminian Clarke hear the deeper graces of justifying righteousness, peace with God, and spiritual joy. Read either way, the point stands: the realities the kingdom is made of dwarf any dispute over food.

He that in these things serveth Christ is acceptable to God, and approved of men. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the double commendation — he who makes righteousness, peace, and joy the substance of his service, rather than food-disputes, truly serves his Master and is well-pleasing to God. reformed Calvin adds that such a life wins even the honest judgment of men, who cannot but esteem a peaceable and righteous walk.

Let us therefore follow after the things which make for peace, and things wherewith one may edify another. grammatical-historical Barnes and puritan Matthew Henry set the two aims side by side — peace and edification — as the measure by which the use of liberty is to be judged. reformed Calvin and eastern Theodoret make the rule plain: whatever wrecks concord or hinders a brother’s building-up is to be refused, however lawful in itself.

For meat destroy not the work of God. All things indeed are pure; but it is evil for that man who eateth with offence. grammatical-historical Robertson takes “the work of God” as the brother himself — the soul God is building — which the strong must not pull down for a meal. reformed Calvin marks the balance: the principle is conceded (all foods are clean), yet the sin lies not in the food but in the loveless use that trips a brother.

It is good neither to eat flesh, nor to drink wine, nor any thing whereby thy brother stumbleth, or is offended, or is made weak. grammatical-historical Barnes notes the gradation — to stumble, to be scandalized, to have one’s faith injured — any of which the strong should spare the weak. wesleyan-arminian Wesley and eastern Theodoret widen the rule beyond food to anything indifferent: love willingly lays down its own rights for a brother’s sake.

Hast thou faith? have it to thyself before God. Happy is he that condemneth not himself in that thing which he alloweth. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read “faith” here as the strong man’s settled persuasion that all is lawful — to be enjoyed privately before God, not paraded where it wounds. reformed Calvin dwells on the beatitude: happy the man whose conscience does not reproach him in what he approves and does.

He that doubteth is damned if he eat, because he eateth not of faith: for whatsoever is not of faith is sin. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott read the great maxim narrowly of the case in hand — whatever is done without the conviction that it is right is sin — expressly resisting Augustine’s use of it to prove every act of an unbeliever sinful. wesleyan-arminian Clarke grants the immediate sense is the doubting conscience, yet notes the broader Augustinian reading enshrined in the Church’s Article XIII. Both agree the practical rule: a conscience must never be violated; to act in doubt is to sin.

The chapter ends not in argument but in worship: Now to him that is of power to stablish you according to my gospel. eastern Chrysostom notes it is Paul’s habit to close an exhortation with a doxology, and hears in “stablish you” a last tenderness toward the very weak brethren — they stood, but with wavering, and he prays God to fix them fast. The establishing comes “according to my gospel and the preaching of Jesus Christ”: it is Christ’s own message, not human rule, that finally settles a trembling conscience.

witnesses.point: The strong man’s principle is true — nothing is unclean of itself — but love must govern its exercise; “the work of God” is the brother whom a careless liberty can pull down.
“Destroy not” warns of the ruinous tendency of wounding the weak, though God preserves his own; the sin lies not in the food but in the uncharitable use of a real liberty.

One for whom Christ died may actually perish (v.15); and “whatsoever is not of faith is sin” carries, beyond the doubting conscience, the broader truth that works without faith have the nature of sin.
A mistaken conscience still binds until better taught; and the closing doxology returns in tenderness to the weak, praying God to establish those who as yet stand with wavering.

external.name: Douglas J. Moo
external.point: The strong are theologically correct that no food is intrinsically unclean, yet Paul subordinates the right exercise of freedom to love: a freedom that wounds the weak believer’s conscience must be voluntarily restrained, for the kingdom’s true goods are righteousness, peace, and joy, not diet.

13.application: Freedom is real, but it is always exercised in community, and love sets its limit exactly where liberty begins to destroy. Where your conscience is clear, keep it clear before God — and be willing to lay a lawful thing down rather than trip someone Christ died to save.

_meta.title: Welcome the One Who Is Weak in Faith
reflection: Paul tells the church to welcome the one who is weak in faith, because to his own master he stands or falls. Where are you tempted to make a secondary conviction a test of who really belongs?

In a passage that forbids judging, Paul still insists each of us will give account of himself to God (v.10). How do “do not judge” and “you are accountable” belong together rather than cancel each other out?

The strong are charged not to destroy with their food the one for whom Christ died, but to pursue what makes for peace. Is there a freedom you could gladly lay down for a brother or sister whose conscience is not yet free?
